questions.question: Under the reign of Islamic rulers, which among the following used to be a private building?
questions.solution:
Private vs. Public Buildings

Under Islamic rulers, architecture was often divided by function. The Diwan-i-Khas was a private audience hall used by the Emperor to meet with high-ranking officials and dignitaries in confidence.

  • Diwan-i-Aam: The public audience hall for common citizens.
  • Sarai: A public inn for travelers.
  • Katra: A market enclosure or wholesale market.

questions.question: The famous building 'Qutub Minar' has been built under which style of architecture?
questions.solution:
Mamluk Style Architecture

The Qutub Minar was initiated by Qutub-ud-din Aibak, the founder of the Mamluk (Slave) Dynasty. This style represents the early Indo-Islamic phase, characterized by the use of red sandstone, intricate carvings of Quranic verses, and the use of the corbelled arch technique.

questions.question: The Dutch East India Company's trade on the Coromandel Coast registered a significant increase over the 17ᵗʰ century due to:
questions.option_1: the decline and collapse of the British influence in the region
questions.option_2: an increase in the demand for textile from the south-east Asian markets
questions.option_3: the establishment of trading posts in Goa and Surat
questions.solution:
Dutch Trade on Coromandel Coast

The Dutch East India Company (VOC) saw a surge in trade on the Coromandel Coast because of the massive demand for Indian textiles in the South-East Asian markets (like Indonesia). The Dutch traded these textiles for valuable spices, creating a profitable triangular trade route.

questions.question: 'Adhai Din Ka Jhonpra' in Ajmer was built in the period of which of the following dynasties?
questions.solution:
Adhai Din Ka Jhonpra

Located in Ajmer, this structure was originally a Sanskrit college that was converted into a mosque by Qutub-ud-din Aibak of the Slave Dynasty. It is one of the oldest mosques in India and is famous for its rapid construction, which legend says took only 'two and a half days'.

questions.question: The Maitrakas of Valabhi were initially the:
questions.solution:
The Maitrakas

The Maitrakas of Vallabhi (Gujarat) were originally military subordinates of the Gupta Empire. As the central authority of the Guptas weakened in the late 5th century, the Maitrakas declared their independence and established a powerful regional kingdom.

questions.question: In 1610, the Dutch established a factory in which of the following places, which later became the headquarters of the Dutch directorate for the Coromandel region?
questions.solution:
Dutch Headquarters at Pulicat

In 1610, the Dutch founded a factory at Pulicat (Tamil Nadu). They built Fort Geldria there, which served as the headquarters for the Dutch Coromandel directorate until it was eventually moved to Nagapattinam in 1690.

questions.question: The Harsha inscription of 554 CE mentions the rise of Yajnavarman from which place?
questions.solution:
Rise of Yajnavarman

The Harsha inscription (dating to 554 CE) mentions the rise of the Maukhari ruler Yajnavarman from Gaya. The Maukharis were an influential family that eventually established their power at Kannauj, becoming key rivals to the later Guptas.

questions.question: The Recorder’s Court was later replaced by the ______.
questions.solution:
Evolution of Judiciary

The Recorder’s Court, which existed in the presidencies of Madras and Bombay, was later replaced by the Supreme Court. Eventually, the Indian High Courts Act of 1861 established High Courts, consolidating the judicial system further.

questions.question: In March 2025, the Union Home Minister inaugurated the Lachit Barphukan Police Academy in Assam to honour his legacy. Who was Lachit Barphukan?
questions.option_1: A commander of the Ahom kingdom
questions.option_4: A military commander of the Mughals
questions.solution:
Lachit Barphukan

Lachit Barphukan was a legendary commander of the Ahom kingdom in Assam. He is most famous for leading the Ahom forces in the Battle of Saraighat (1671), where he successfully repelled a massive Mughal invasion led by Ram Singh I.

questions.question: ​​​​​​Which ruler regained his lost kingdom by 1555 after defeating Sher Shah Suri's successors, thereby marking the end of the Second Afghan Empire?
questions.solution:
Restoration of Humayun

The Mughal Emperor Humayun regained his throne in 1555. After spending 15 years in exile following his defeat by Sher Shah Suri, he took advantage of the internal strife among the successors of the Suri dynasty (the Second Afghan Empire) to reclaim Delhi.

questions.question: Which ruler, upon his death in 1530, left behind a newly established empire that stretched across northern India—from the Indus River in the west to Bihar in the east, and from the Himalayas in the north to Gwalior in the south?
questions.solution:
The Empire of Babur

At the time of his death in 1530, Babur had established the Mughal Empire. After his victory at the First Battle of Panipat (1526) and the Battle of Khanwa (1527), his domain stretched from the Indus to Bihar, forming the foundation for the massive empire his successors would build.

questions.question: Which princely ruler patronised the Sikh community and institutions?
questions.solution:
Patiala and the Sikh Community

Maharaja Bhupinder Singh of Patiala was a prominent princely ruler who significantly patronized Sikh institutions. He served as the first Chancellor of the Chamber of Princes and was instrumental in the religious and social affairs of the Sikh community in the early 20th century.

questions.question: During which dynasty did the Mongols, led by Qultlug Khwaja, besiege Delhi and inflict significant damage?
questions.solution:
Mongol Siege under the Khaljis

During the reign of Alauddin Khalji, the Mongols under Qutlugh Khwaja launched a massive invasion in 1299, reaching the outskirts of Delhi. This threat forced Alauddin to implement significant military and economic reforms (market controls) to maintain a large standing army.

questions.question: Mihira Bhoja, a famous ruler of the Pratiharas, adopted which title?
questions.solution:
Mihira Bhoja's Title

Mihira Bhoja, the most powerful ruler of the Gurjara-Pratihara dynasty, was a devotee of Lord Vishnu. He adopted the title Adivaraha (The First Boar), and his coins featured the image of the Varaha incarnation of Vishnu.

questions.question: Which of the following architectures has been built in the 'Charbagh' style?
questions.solution:
Charbagh Style

The Taj Mahal is a classic example of the Charbagh (Four Gardens) style. This Persian-inspired layout divides a quadrilateral garden into four smaller parts using walkways or flowing water, symbolizing the gardens of Paradise.

questions.question: Who among the following appointed a superintendent (Shahna-i-Mandi) for the effective implementation of market reforms?
questions.solution:
Market Reforms of Alauddin Khalji

Alauddin Khalji introduced strict market regulations to keep the prices of essential goods low. To enforce this, he appointed a Shahna-i-Mandi (Market Superintendent) who was responsible for checking weights, measures, and preventing black marketing.

questions.question: The tripartite struggle for Kannauj was among the:
questions.solution:
The Tripartite Struggle

The Tripartite Struggle was a long-standing conflict during the 8th and 9th centuries for the control of Kannauj, the symbol of sovereignty in North India. The three powers involved were the Palas (East), the Gurjara-Pratiharas (West/North), and the Rashtrakutas (Deccan).

questions.question: Which Delhi Sultanate ruler was associated with the expedition of Bhilsa (Vidisha) in AD 1292?
questions.solution:
Expedition to Bhilsa

In 1292, Alauddin Khalji (then a governor under his uncle Jalaluddin Khalji) led a successful military expedition to Bhilsa (Vidisha). The massive wealth looted from this campaign helped him eventually seize the throne of Delhi.

questions.question: Which of the following materials has been mainly used for building the Jahangiri Mahal in Agra Fort?
questions.solution:
Jahangiri Mahal

The Jahangiri Mahal, located inside the Agra Fort, was built primarily using red sandstone. Constructed during Akbar's reign for his son Jahangir, it features a unique blend of Hindu and Islamic architectural elements.

questions.question: Which of the following architectures was built by Aurangzeb?
questions.solution:
Bibi-ka-Maqbara

The Bibi-ka-Maqbara in Aurangabad was built by Aurangzeb in memory of his first wife, Dilras Banu Begum. It is often called the 'Taj of the Deccan' because its design was heavily inspired by the Taj Mahal.

questions.question: Which ruler of the Delhi Sultanate was associated with the expedition to Bhilsa (Vidisha) in AD 1292?
questions.solution:
Alauddin Khalji and Vidisha

As noted in the previous context, Alauddin Khalji's 1292 expedition to Bhilsa (Vidisha) was a turning point in his career, providing him with the resources to fund his later Deccan campaigns and his coup against the Sultan.

questions.question: Which group gained increased importance in the post-Gupta society due to land grants?
questions.solution:
Social Change through Land Grants

In the post-Gupta period, the practice of giving land grants (Agraharas) to Brahmins led to their increased political and economic importance. They became intermediaries between the king and the peasants, effectively becoming part of a new landed aristocracy.

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Gurjara-Pratihara dynasty?
questions.solution:
Founder of the Gurjara-Pratiharas

Nagabhata I is recognized as the founder of the powerful Gurjara-Pratihara dynasty in the 8th century. He is credited with successfully resisting Arab invasions from Sindh, protecting the Indian heartland.

questions.question: ’Top-khanah’ was the general name given to the military/artillery branch of which of the following?
questions.solution:
Mughal Top-khanah

The Top-khanah refers to the artillery department of the Mughal Dynasty. Introduced significantly by Babur at the Battle of Panipat, gunpowder and cannons became the decisive 'game-changer' in Mughal military superiority.

questions.question: In which of the following buildings/towns constructed by the Mughals is Salim Chishti's tomb situated?
questions.solution:
Tomb of Salim Chishti

The white marble tomb of the Sufi saint Salim Chishti is located within the courtyard of the Jama Masjid at Fatehpur Sikri. It was built by Akbar as a mark of respect for the saint who had predicted the birth of his son, Jahangir.

questions.question: What was the name of the new department opened by Muhammad Tughlaq for the development of agriculture?
questions.solution:
Diwan-i-Kohi

Muhammad bin Tughlaq established a dedicated department for agriculture called Diwan-i-Amir-i-Kohi. Its goal was to bring uncultivated land under the plow by providing state loans (sondhar) to farmers.

questions.question: What was the name of the new department opened by Muhammad Tughlaq for the development of agriculture?
questions.solution:
Agricultural Department (Repeated)

As mentioned, the Diwan-i-Kohi was the department created to oversee agricultural expansion and reform under Muhammad bin Tughlaq's administration.

questions.question: In 1510, Alfonzo de Albuquerque captured which place from the Sultan of Bijapur and made it the capital of the Portuguese eastern empire?
questions.solution:
Capture of Goa

In 1510, the Portuguese Admiral Afonso de Albuquerque captured Goa from the Sultan of Bijapur (Ismail Adil Shah). Goa subsequently became the political and cultural capital of the Portuguese State of India (Estado da Índia).

questions.question: The term 'Bhukti' in post-Gupta polity referred to:
questions.solution:
Administrative Units: Bhukti

In the administrative hierarchy of the Gupta and post-Gupta periods, a Bhukti was a province. It was further divided into Vishayas (districts). The head of a Bhukti was usually called an Uparika.

questions.question: The Rashtrakutas dominated which part of India after the Guptas?
questions.solution:
The Rashtrakutas

The Rashtrakutas rose to power in the Deccan (central-southern India) in the 8th century, succeeding the Chalukyas of Badami. They were famous for their patronage of art, including the Kailasa Temple at Ellora.

questions.question: ‘Tuhfat-ul-Muwahhidin’ was written by:
questions.solution:
Tuhfat-ul-Muwahhidin

Raja Rammohan Roy, the 'Father of the Indian Renaissance,' wrote Tuhfat-ul-Muwahhidin (A Gift to Monotheists) in 1803. In this Persian work, he argued against idolatry and for the belief in a single universal God.

questions.question: The Ramoshis who worked for 'Ramoshi Uprising' were night guards protecting the forts of which community?
questions.solution:
Ramoshi Uprising

The Ramoshis were traditionally night guards for the Maratha forts. Following the fall of the Maratha Empire and the subsequent loss of their livelihoods under British rule, they rose in rebellion under leaders like Chittur Singh and Vasudev Balwant Phadke.

questions.question: In one of his famous works, Yahya bin Ahmad Sirhindi claims that the founder of the _____ was a descendant of the Prophet Muhammad.
questions.solution:
Descent of the Sayyid Dynasty

The historian Yahya bin Ahmad Sirhindi, author of Tarikh-i-Mubarak Shahi, claimed that the founder of the Sayyid Dynasty (Khizr Khan) was a descendant of the Prophet Muhammad. This claim helped the dynasty gain religious legitimacy after the chaos of Timur's invasion.

questions.question: In one of his famous works, Yahya bin Ahmad Sirhindi claims that the founder of _____ was a descendant of the Prophet.
questions.solution:
Sayyid Genealogy (Repeated)

The Sayyid Dynasty rulers styled themselves as 'Sayyids' to assert their status as direct descendants of the Prophet's family, as documented by contemporary chroniclers like Yahya bin Ahmad Sirhindi.
